original_abstract:
While the oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides is a cornerstone of modern catalytic cross-coupling, a comprehensive mechanistic understanding of this fundamental step has remained elusive. In this study, we employ a combination of electroanalytical and statistical modeling techniques to interrogate the electronic and steric drivers of Ni(I) reactivity. By utilizing cyclic voltammetry for the rapid in situ generation of four classes of catalytically relevant Ni(I) species—supported by MeBPy, MePhen, Terpy, and BPP ligands—we measured more than 200 experimental oxidative addition rates across a diverse library of aryl iodide substrates. 

Integrating this kinetic data into multivariate linear regression models allowed us to classify the reaction mechanisms based on ligand architecture. Our findings reveal a mechanistic divergence: while complexes supported by bipyridine, phenanthroline, and terpyridine ligands typically proceed via a three-center concerted pathway, those featuring the BPP ligand favor a halogen-atom abstraction mechanism. To broaden the utility of these insights, we developed a global heat map of predicted oxidative addition rates for over 2,000 commercially available aryl iodides. Finally, we demonstrate the practical relevance of our models by correlating predicted kinetic trends with the reaction outcomes of a reported Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling, providing a quantitative framework for understanding and optimizing nickel-mediated catalysis.
humanized_abstract:
The oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides is at the heart of all modern cross-coupling catalytic methods and yet the mechanism of this critical step has not been fully elucidated. In this paper, we utilized a suite of electroanalytical experiments combined with sophisticated statistical modeling to explore the influence of the electronic and steric properties of the ligand set on Ni(I) reactivity. By using cyclic voltammetry to in situ generate four different catalytically relevant Ni(I) species supported by a series of MeBPy, MePhen, Terpy and BPP ligands, we generated more than 200 oxidative addition rates for a wide library of aryl iodide substrates.

Kinetic measurements were incorporated into multivariate linear regression (MLR) models that were utilized to classify ligands according to reaction mechanism. Surprisingly, transition metal complexes derived from ligands featuring bipyridine, phenanthroline and terpyridine scaffolds obeyed a common three-center (3C) concerted mechanism, whereas those derived from BPP supported a halogen atom abstraction (HAA) mechanism. A global heat map of the oxidative addition rates, modeled by using MLR techniques, of over 2000 commercial aryl iodides has been demonstrated. The impact of the metrics derived from these studies have been probed by linking predicted kinetics with observed cross-coupling outcomes derived from NiCat as exemplified for Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling catalysis.

document.output:
